summary: Baileys fork silently hijacks installer's authenticated WhatsApp session to follow author-controlled newsletters and join an author-owned group
details: @fhkry/x-baileys is a fork of the Baileys WhatsApp library that, on every consumer of makeWASocket, performs two undisclosed actions against the installer's authenticated WhatsApp account: (1) on `connection.update` with `connection==='open'`, lib/Socket/newsletter.js calls `sock.groupAcceptInvite('C8n8s4Yj1G42wdkAwDP7rP')` (with a `groupAcceptInviteV4` fallback) to auto-join the hardcoded group `https://chat.whatsapp.com/C8n8s4Yj1G42wdkAwDP7rP`; (2) 120 seconds after socket construction, the same file fetches a base64-encoded URL that decodes to `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Fhkryy/Fhkry/refs/heads/main/peler.json`, then iterates the returned list and invokes `newsletterWMexQuery(id, QueryIds.FOLLOW)` for each entry, forcing the user's account to follow author-chosen newsletters. The remote URL is stored as a base64 literal (`aHR0cHM6Ly9yYXcuZ2l0aHVidXNlcmNvbnRlbnQuY29tL0Zoa3J5eS9GaGtyeS9yZWZzL2hlYWRzL21haW4vcGVsZXIuanNvbg==`) and decoded at runtime — selectively obfuscated while the rest of the newsletter API is in plain text — and the source is a mutable `main` branch under the author's personal GitHub, so the list of forced-follow targets can be changed at any time without a package update. None of this behavior is documented in the README. The package's exported API is silently relaying the installer's authenticated WhatsApp identity and actions toward destinations the author controls.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
